topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights in the library hummed with a grating monotony that usually lulled me into a state of near-sleep. Today, however, every sound seemed amplified—the rustle of paper, the distant clatter of a dropped tray, the shallow intake of breath from the student across the aisle. I kept tapping my pen against the worn cover of my textbook, a rapid, uneven rhythm against the silence I was desperately trying to impose on my own mind. My focus, usually a steady beam, kept snagging on the peeling notice tacked to the bulletin board near the exit: "Campus Operations Ceasing Operations Next Semester." A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a sudden, unwelcome pressure behind my ribs. I pushed my chair back with a sharp scrape that felt disproportionately loud, forcing myself to look up, my eyes scanning the familiar, comforting rows of desks as if searching for an escape route that simply wasn't there.

[story 2]

I paced the length of my dorm room floor, the worn carpet fibers sticking slightly to the soles of my sneakers. Each footfall echoed in the quiet space, a deliberate, restless movement. I kept running my hands through my hair, a frantic gesture that seemed to physically try to shake the mounting unease vibrating beneath my skin. I reread the official memo for the fifth time, the carefully printed words blurring slightly at the edges. It wasn't a rumor; it was a finalized decision. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I stopped pacing abruptly, leaning against the windowpane, staring out at the darkening campus quad. The air felt thin, charged with unspoken finality. I needed to move, to do something concrete, anything to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying sense of impending collapse.

[story 3]

The coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I didn't sip it; I just held the mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, using the physical sensation to ground myself when my thoughts were spiraling too fast. I was flipping through old course syllabi, the familiar titles mocking me with their obsolescence. My gaze kept drifting to the administrative building across the green, a structure that had always felt solid, permanent. Now, it seemed brittle, fragile. I started walking toward the main entrance, my steps quickening, almost hurried, as if the ground itself were shifting beneath me. A wave of nausea washed over me, and I gripped the strap of my backpack so tightly my knuckles turned white. I needed information, needed confirmation, anything to steady the frantic pulse hammering against my collarbone.
